mappings.rationaleAI: Section A.1 covers the ideal of paideia, Hellenistic scholarship, and the classical tradition that defines humanistic learning.
Section A.1.a covers Homeric education and the ideal of the hero as the beginning of learning.
Section A.1.b covers the Sophists and Socrates and the turn to logos, the beginnings of rhetoric.
Section A.1.c covers Plato and the Academy, the relation of theology to mythology, and mathematics in service of philosophy.
Section A.1.d covers Aristotle and the Lyceum, the invention of logic, and the division and organization of the sciences.
Sections A.1.b-e cover the philosophical foundations of humanistic learning from the Sophists through Hellenistic scholarship.
Section A.1.b covers the beginnings of rhetoric, A.1.f the Roman ideal of training the orator, and A.1.g the conflict among rhetoric, philosophy, and science.
Classical literature is foundational to the humanistic tradition from Hellenistic literary criticism (A.1.e) through the quarrel of ancients and moderns (A.3.e).
Section A.1.e covers the development of literary and textual criticism in Hellenistic scholarship, foundational to modern literary theory.
Section A.2.a covers the Christianization of pagan culture and reconciliation of classical humanism with Christian revelation.
Section A.2.d covers the Scholastic method and the distinction of philosophy from sacred theology.
Section A.2 covers Christian learning in the Middle Ages including liberal arts, the trivium and quadrivium, and the Scholastic method.
Late Antiquity covers the transition from pagan classical learning to Christian scholarship (A.2.a), pivotal for humanistic scholarship.
Section A.3.a covers the idea of Renaissance including the ideal of the classical, the rise of the vernacular, and the dignity of the individual.
The Renaissance (A.3.a) saw art as an expression of humanistic ideals about the classical and human dignity.
Section A.3.b covers humanism and the new learning as a key development in the history of humanistic scholarship.
Machiavelli exemplifies Renaissance humanism (A.3.b) and the development of political thought within the humanistic tradition.
Montaigne's essays exemplify Renaissance humanism (A.3.a-b) and humanistic forms of philosophical and literary expression.
Dante stands at the transition from medieval to Renaissance learning (A.2 to A.3), bridging Scholastic method and humanistic ideals.
Goethe represents the ideal of Bildung and humanistic learning, embodying the development of humane letters (A.3).
Section A.3.c-d covers the search for a universal method and the separation of science from philosophy.
Philosophy is central to the humanities (B.1-3) and the history of learning from Plato's Academy through the quarrel of ancients and moderns.
Section A.5 covers the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship, its nature, methods, and relation to classical learning.
Section A.6 covers humanities and humanistic scholarship in the East, including China.
Section A.6 covers humanities and humanistic scholarship in the East including India.
Section A.4 covers the Jewish tradition of humanistic scholarship, its nature, methods, and development.
Biblical studies connect to the Jewish (A.4) and Christian (A.2) traditions of humanistic scholarship and textual criticism.
Sections A.2.a-e cover the relationship between theology and humanistic learning, including faith and reason.
Hermeneutics addresses text interpretation, central to humanistic scholarship from Hellenistic literary criticism (A.1.e) through modern methods.
Section A.3.g covers the rise of the liberal arts college and graduate school, and B.2 covers the humanities as an educational program.
Section A.2.c covers the founding of universities, and A.3.g-i cover the contemporary university and profession of learning.
English literature is a major humanistic discipline within the growth of special disciplines (A.3.h).
Comparative literature is a humanistic discipline exemplifying the growth of special disciplines (A.3.h).
Art history is a humanistic discipline connected to the development of scholarship from the Renaissance through modern methods.
Art theory addresses the philosophical and critical dimensions of the humanities and artistic expression.
Aesthetics is a humanistic discipline connecting philosophy, art, and literature, relevant to B.1-3.
Critical theory is a major approach within the humanities (B.1-3) engaging with humanities as a field of knowledge.
Postmodernism addresses the question of the humanities as a unified field of study (B.2) and challenges traditional assumptions.
Poststructuralism is a theoretical approach within the humanities challenging traditional humanistic scholarship.
Modernism transformed humanistic expression and scholarship in the 20th century.
Continental philosophy represents a major tradition within the humanities engaging with hermeneutics, phenomenology, and critical theory.
Existentialism is a 20th-century philosophical movement within the humanities concerned with human existence and meaning.
The Presocratics represent the very beginnings of learning (A.1) from which the humanistic tradition emerged.
Writing is fundamental to humanistic scholarship, connected to literary and textual criticism (A.1.e).
Reading connects to the humanities as an educational program (B.2) and humanistic engagement with texts.
